original_hypothesis.title: PairFactory referral fee misconfiguration can permanently DoS swaps via underflow in Pair fee update
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: PairFactory.setReferralFee and setCustomReferralFee have no bounds enforcement. If MAX_REFERRAL_FEE or customReferralFees[pair] is set above 10000, Pair._update0/_update1 computes referralFee = amount * maxRef / 10000 which becomes > amount, then executes `amount -= _referralFee`, underflowing and reverting every swap that tries to accrue fees. This halts trading protocol-wide for affected pairs (potentially all pairs if MAX_REFERRAL_FEE is used).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a direct invariant violation (INV-ACCESS-001). While exploitation requires privileged feeManager control (thus LOW realism under the bright-line rule), it is operationally critical because it can be triggered accidentally during routine parameter changes or through governance/role mistakes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(_refFee <= 10000); Pair._update0/_update1 does `amount -= _referralFee` where _referralFee = amount * maxRef / 10000

preconditions: feeManager (or genesisManager via setGenesisStatus path is irrelevant here) sets referral fee > 10000 globally or for a specific pair
Swaps occur on impacted pairs (fee accrual path executed)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fee parameter setter lacks basic bounds; Pair fee accrual uses subtraction that will underflow
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time fee parameters are updated

operational_questions: What governance/process controls exist around fee changes (review, simulation, staging)?
Are there automated parameter sanity checks in deployment/ops tooling?
Can customReferralFees be set on per-pair basis widely and accidentally brick specific markets?
validation_requirements: Unit test setReferralFee(10001) then perform Pair.swap and confirm revert
Check current production values for MAX_REFERRAL_FEE/customReferralFees
Review ops scripts for parameter setting and whether they validate bounds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Acquire feeManager privileges (out of bright-line scope) or trigger misconfiguration
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Observe all swaps reverting on affected pairs due to underflow in Pair._update0/_update1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit market disruption elsewhere (secondary economic effects)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Global control over swap availability via parameter mis-set
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol-wide trading halt for v2 pairs; liquidity stranded in pools
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (requires fee reset by feeManager; may cause user panic)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All Pair.swap calls on impacted pairs

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low (single admin tx) but requires privilege

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Fee parameter changes; swaps reverting with arithmetic underflow
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Fee updates not accompanied by change-management artifacts

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
